Q1: I tried to keep things organized, but honestly it was a struggle sometimes. I usually set aside time after my extracurricular activities to study, maybe two hours in the evening most days. Being a kinesthetic learner, I found I needed to physically write things out or practice problems hands-on, so my routine involved a lot of notebook work alongside the digital materials. I aimed for about 14 hours a week total, spread across different days depending on what was due.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines and what else was going on in my life. I tried to have some regularity, like studying on Tuesday and Thursday evenings, but when assignments piled up or my anxiety spiked, everything shifted. Sometimes I'd have to cram more hours into a weekend because I'd been too stressed to focus during the week. The mindfulness exercises I started doing helped me get back on track when things got chaotic.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I knew there was something due soon or when I wanted to check if grades had been posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to watch the lecture videos while doing other things, like folding laundry or eating. The notifications on my phone also prompted me to check in, especially when discussion posts were active. I'd say anxiety about missing something important was often the real motivator.
Q4: Yeah, after the first few weeks, checking the LMS in the morning became kind of automatic for me. I'd wake up, check my phone, and open the app to see if anything new was posted. It became part of my routine like checking social media. The high engagement kind of happened naturally because I was always worried I'd miss an announcement or deadline, so I just kept logging in.
Q5: The video lectures were honestly the most helpful for me because I could pause them and take physical notes at my own pace. I watched over 270 minutes of video content because I'd often rewatch sections I didn't understand the first time. The discussion boards were also useful when other students asked questions I hadn't thought of—it made me feel less alone in my confusion. Anything I could interact with actively felt more valuable than just reading.
Q6: I'll be honest, I didn't complete all the readings or some of the supplementary materials. With limited resources at home, I couldn't always print things out, and reading long PDFs on my phone screen gave me headaches. Some of the optional practice quizzes I skipped because I was already overwhelmed with the required work. When my anxiety was high, I'd avoid anything that felt like it would add more stress.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it directly connected to the assignments or exams. If I could physically do something with the material—like work through practice problems or create study cards—it felt productive. I also valued activities where I could see immediate progress or get feedback. Anything that felt too abstract or disconnected from the assessments seemed like a waste of my limited energy.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford. With my motivation being kind of low and my stress levels high, I had to prioritize what was going to count toward my grade. Required activities had this urgency that pushed me through even when I didn't feel like it. Optional stuff was easier to push aside when I was anxious or tired, even if I knew it might help me learn better.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download the lecture slides and print them at the library when I could afford it, then study from those physical copies. I also borrowed textbooks from friends since I couldn't buy all of them myself. Writing out notes by hand and doing practice problems on paper was how I really learned—that kinesthetic approach doesn't show up in any system.
Q10: There were many times I was studying intensely but the LMS wouldn't know. I'd watch a video once, download the slides, and then spend hours working through the material with my notebook at the kitchen table. Group study sessions with classmates happened in person, not online. My peer interactions number only shows 15, but I actually studied with friends in person way more than that suggests.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a lecture video or PDF once, download it or take screenshots, and then never open it again in the LMS. My phone storage was full of course materials I'd saved for offline studying. This was partly because my internet at home isn't always reliable, so I needed to have things accessible without being online. The LMS would show one view, but I might have studied that material five or six times.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially around midterms when everything seemed due at once. My anxiety would spike and I'd feel paralyzed, unable to start anything. Some weeks my extracurricular commitments got intense and I had less energy for coursework. The mindfulness intervention actually helped me push through some of those harder moments—it gave me tools to calm down and refocus.
Q13: Oh definitely. When I was exhausted or stressed, I'd log into the LMS but just kind of scroll without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities—like helping out at home since money is tight—sometimes meant I couldn't engage as deeply as I wanted. Confusion about assignment requirements also made me avoid the LMS because looking at unclear instructions just made my anxiety worse. Fatigue was probably my biggest enemy.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd have the LMS open and be watching videos but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd be worrying about other things or just too tired to process what I was seeing. Sometimes I'd read the same paragraph three times and still not remember what it said. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than actually learning anything meaningful.
Q15: I definitely postponed assignments even when I knew they were important. My assignment completion rate wasn't great because sometimes the anxiety of starting something felt worse than the anxiety of it being late. I'd avoid tasks that seemed overwhelming or confusing, telling myself I'd do them when I felt better. The irony is that waiting usually made my anxiety worse, but in the moment, avoidance felt like survival.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my LMS usage. I'd be pretty quiet on the platform until a deadline approached, then suddenly I'd be logging in multiple times a day, rewatching videos, checking requirements. The pressure of a deadline could cut through my avoidance and anxiety in a way that nothing else could. It wasn't healthy, but that urgency was sometimes the only thing that got me to submit work.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Positive feedback boosted my mood and made me want to engage more—it felt like validation that I could do this. But critical feedback sometimes triggered my anxiety and made me avoid that part of the course for a while. I needed time to process criticism before I could use it constructively. The timing of feedback mattered a lot for my emotional state.
Q18: The course structure helped in some ways because I knew what to expect each week. Having a predictable pattern made it easier to plan around my other commitments. But when multiple things were due at once, the structure felt suffocating rather than supportive. I wished there was more flexibility for students like me who were dealing with high anxiety and limited resources.
Q19: The video content being available anytime was a huge help because I could study when my anxiety was lower, even if that was 2 AM. But the discussion requirements felt harder because I had to be present at certain times to get meaningful engagement. The assignment instructions could have been clearer—vague requirements increased my anxiety significantly. More hands-on, practical activities would have suited my learning style better.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious and keeps checking things without actually being able to focus and complete work. You might log in repeatedly hoping you'll feel ready to start, but the anxiety keeps blocking you. Or maybe someone is struggling to understand the material, so they keep reviewing but can't produce quality submissions. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity when your mental state is working against you.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. My exam score was decent even though my assignment completion wasn't great. Some students might be studying effectively offline—like I did with printed materials and handwritten notes—so their learning doesn't show in the LMS. Or they might be naturally good at exams but struggle with the sustained effort assignments require. The LMS only captures one slice of the learning picture.
Q22: I think course logs can accurately show patterns like when someone logs in, how often, and what they click on. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement—whether someone is actually learning or just scrolling anxiously. They miss offline studying, emotional states, and the real reasons behind behaviors. A log might show I watched a video, but it can't show that I was crying from stress while watching it.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about the material and wanting to understand it, not just pass. It's when I'm actively thinking, questioning, connecting ideas to real life. For me, engagement feels physical too—writing, practicing, doing something with my hands. It's also emotional; when I'm engaged, I feel curious rather than just anxious. True engagement is when learning feels meaningful rather than like a chore I'm forcing myself through.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my anxiety affected every aspect of my engagement—the hours I spent worrying instead of working, the assignments I understood but couldn't bring myself to start. It wouldn't capture the mindfulness practices that helped me get through tough weeks, or the support from friends during in-person study sessions. It also wouldn't show that despite my struggles, I genuinely cared about learning and worked hard in ways that don't leave digital footprints.
